tldr: Two-stage fine-tuning of ByT5-XL on noisy intermediate data followed by high-quality curated pairs, combined with custom PDF extraction pipelines using OCR and VLMs to generate training data.
summary: The solution leverages ByT5-XL for byte-level translation of Akkadian script, trained via a two-stage supervised fine-tuning process: first on ~350k noisy pairs for broad prior learning, then on ~65k high-quality curated pairs for precise alignment. Training data was primarily generated through custom PDF extraction pipelines using GLM-OCR with a sliding window context for regular layouts, and GLM4.7V-Flash for columnar layouts, alongside Mistral 14B and GPT-OSS-120B for publication normalization. The author found cross-validation highly unreliable compared to the leaderboard, relying instead on a fixed 500-sample holdout. Key score drivers included the two-stage fine-tuning schedule and careful data extraction, while techniques like RL-based alignment, LLM post-processing, and extended Stage 2 training proved detrimental.
extensive_summary:
## Model Selection & Architecture
The author selected ByT5-XL as the base architecture, noting that larger models consistently yielded better scores (1-1.5 GM difference). ByT5’s byte-level tokenization was chosen over subword tokenizers (like NLLB or mT5) because Akkadian script does not tokenize cleanly with standard subword approaches.

## Two-Stage Fine-Tuning Strategy
The core methodology relied on a two-stage supervised fine-tuning (SFT) process. Stage 1 involved training on ~350k noisy, unclean Akkadian-English pairs (including single-word dictionary entries and partial sentences) for 3 epochs. Training configuration used a global batch size of 128 (bf16 precision, per-device batch size 16, gradient accumulation of 4, across 2 devices). Stage 2 focused on ~65k high-quality, curated transliteration-to-translation pairs, trained for exactly 1 epoch in fp32. The author explicitly noted that extending Stage 2 beyond one epoch caused model collapse and leaderboard score drops. This combination of broad noisy pretraining followed by clean targeted fine-tuning was identified as the primary driver for surpassing 40 on the LB.

## Data Extraction & Pipeline Engineering
Host recommendations advised against using external Akkadian corpora due to dialect/time period mismatches. Data was sourced from host-provided publications and PDFs.
For publications, the initial pipeline used Mistral 14B to normalize multilingual text (English, French, German, Turkish) into English, generating the ~350k Stage 1 pairs. Later, this was re-engineered using GPT-OSS-120B in fp4 quantization with sophisticated few-shot prompting and a sliding window approach.
PDF extraction required two distinct pipelines due to layout differences:
- *Regular Text Layout:* Translations often appeared on the next page (~40% of documents). The pipeline used GLM-OCR for text extraction, followed by a sliding window feeding (previous, current, next) pages into GPT-OSS-120B with few-shot prompting to bridge page boundaries and align transliterations with translations.
- *Columnar Layout:* Page misalignment was absent, but OCR failed to separate columns, merging sentences. A VLM-based approach was adopted. Qwen3.5-VL 35B initially showed promise but suffered from low throughput. The author pivoted to GLM4.7V-Flash for better throughput. Although Qwen3.5-VL 35B was later optimized with vLLM for acceptable throughput, retraining on its outputs yielded worse LB scores, so GLM4.7V-Flash outputs were retained. All LLM/VLM selections were made via "vibe-testing" due to the lack of clean ground truth for evaluation.

## Validation & Training Observations
Cross-validation proved highly unreliable, showing no consistent correlation with the leaderboard. The author settled on using the first 500 training pairs as a holdout set, which consistently scored 42-43.5 across 40+ models, making it unsuitable for model selection. The entire pipeline was built on a 100% open-source stack, deployable on a single A100 or 2x RTX 6000.
cv_strategy: First 500 pairs from the training set as a holdout; author noted it had no consistent correlation with the leaderboard and was unreliable for model selection.
preprocessing: Sliding window context feeding previous/current/next pages for PDF extraction
Multilingual normalization to English for publications
what_worked: Two-stage SFT (noisy broad prior -> clean targeted)
ByT5-XL byte-level tokenization for Akkadian script
Custom PDF extraction pipelines (GLM-OCR + sliding window for regular layouts, GLM4.7V-Flash for columnar layouts)
GPT-OSS-120B in fp4 quantization with few-shot prompting
what_did_not_work: Dictionary and RAG-based reconstruction for pseudo-labeling
Training Stage 2 for more than 1 epoch
RL-based methods (DPO, minimum risk training, PPO, GRPO)
critical_findings: ByT5's byte-level tokenization is essential for Akkadian script that fails with standard subword tokenizers.
Extending Stage 2 SFT beyond exactly 1 epoch causes model collapse and leaderboard score drops.
CV showed no consistent correlation with LB, making the 500-pair holdout unreliable for model selection.
Qwen3.5-VL 35B outputs yielded worse LB scores than GLM4.7V-Flash despite higher capability, highlighting the need for throughput/practical tuning over raw benchmarks.
